narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your upper-middle family income and plans to attend full-time like most applicants. However, the admissions model predicted a 65.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish level, signaling to the model that your later academic performance might not hold up for the rigors ahead. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also lower than average, added to this concern by suggesting an early pattern of grades not matching top performers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 34 and undergraduate GPA of 3.0 each pulled in the opposite direction, as they were close enough to averages (around 37 and 3.2) to show some promise compared to students who pass. Your family income and full-time status had smaller positive influences too, aligning with profiles of successful bar takers. Overall, compared to typical students who pass, your decile rankings stood out as weaker, outweighing those other solid aspects and tipping the scales toward failure in the model's view. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average or strengthening your academic record in advanced courses to improve those decile rankings. You might also seek mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build a standout narrative for your next application. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift the prediction in your favor. Keep pushing; your journey is just beginning.
